t: Aquinas's Five Ways: Do They Prove God Exists?
d: Aquinas's Five Ways are not about a first cause in the distant past, but a sustainer acting here and now. A deep, fair look at the Quinque Viae.

t: Did the Universe Begin? The Kalam Cosmological Argument
d: A rigorous, fair-minded deep dive into the Kalam cosmological argument: its premises, its history, the science, the best objections, and what it proves.

t: Leibniz's Contingency Argument: Why Anything Exists
d: Leibniz asked why there is something rather than nothing. A rigorous, fair-minded look at the contingency argument, its modern defenders, and its critics.

t: The Argument from Beauty: Does Beauty Point Beyond the World to God?
d: A scholarly, accessible deep-dive into the aesthetic argument for God — beauty in nature, art, and mathematics; Swinburne, Augustine, Balthasar; and the strongest objections answered.

t: The Argument from Consciousness: Does the Inner Light Point to a Mind?
d: Why subjective experience resists physical explanation, and whether finite minds are better explained by a foundational Mind than by matter alone.

t: The Argument from Desire: Does Our Deepest Longing Point to God?
d: C. S. Lewis argued that a longing nothing on earth satisfies points to God. Examining the argument, its history, and the strongest objections.

t: The Argument from Reason: Can a Mindless Universe Account for Thinking Minds?
d: If naturalism explains our minds by non-rational causes, can it trust reason itself? Lewis, Plantinga, and the argument that undercuts its own ground.

t: The Argument from Religious Experience: Does Encountering God Count as Evidence?
d: Can the worldwide testimony of people who say they have met God count as evidence? A careful look at Swinburne, Alston, James, and their critics.

t: The Moral Argument for God: Where Do Right and Wrong Come From?
d: If objective morality exists — and torturing a child for fun is really wrong — what best explains it? A rigorous, fair look at the moral argument.

t: The Ontological Argument: Can Pure Thought Prove God?
d: From Anselm to Plantinga — the boldest, most contested proof of God: that the greatest conceivable being must exist. Its power, and its honest limits.

t: Is the Universe Fine-Tuned for Life? The Fine-Tuning Argument for God
d: The universe's constants fall in startlingly narrow life-permitting ranges. Is it chance, necessity, or design? A rigorous, fair-minded examination.

t: Why Would a Good God Allow So Much Suffering? The Problem of Evil
d: An honest, humane look at the strongest objection to belief in God: the logical, evidential, and existential problem of evil, and what Christian faith can and cannot say.

t: 1 Corinthians 15:3-7: The Earliest Resurrection Creed
d: How critical scholars across the spectrum date the resurrection creed of 1 Corinthians 15:3-7 to within a few years of the cross — and what that proves.

t: Daniel's Seventy Weeks: Does the Prophecy Point to Jesus?
d: Daniel 9:24-27 seems to time the Messiah's coming to the century of Jesus. How strong is the argument — and where does honest scholarship push back?

t: Did Jesus Claim to Be God? The Historical Case from His Own Words and Deeds
d: A scholarly look at whether the historical Jesus claimed divine identity, building on the implicit claims in the earliest Gospel layers.

t: Did Jesus of Nazareth Really Exist? The Historical Evidence
d: A scholarly look at why nearly all historians, believing and not, hold that Jesus existed, and why mythicism remains a fringe position.

t: Did Jesus Predict His Own Resurrection?
d: Did Jesus foretell his death and rising? A careful look at the passion predictions, the temple saying, and the critics who call them prophecy after the fact.

t: Is Jesus God in the New Testament? The Whole Witness, Surveyed
d: A synthesis of the New Testament's witness to Christ's deity — John, Paul, Hebrews, Revelation, and worship — through early high Christology.

t: John 1:1 Explained: Is the Word God?
d: What John 1:1 really says about Christ's eternity and deity — the Greek, the Logos background, and a fair answer to the "a god" reading and Ehrman.

t: Lord, Liar, or Lunatic? The Trilemma and Jesus's Claims
d: Can a merely good man have claimed what Jesus claimed? Lewis's trilemma, the "legend" objection, and what the argument from Jesus's character proves.

t: Messianic Prophecy: Did Jesus Fulfill the Old Testament?
d: A rigorous, honest tour of the messianic prophecies and their fulfillment in Jesus — the strongest and weakest cases, the textual debates, and what they show.

t: Christ in All the Scriptures: How the Old Testament Foreshadows Jesus Through Typology
d: Not prediction but pattern — how the persons, events, and institutions of the Old Testament foreshadow Christ, with the New Testament's own warrant and its honest limits.

t: Multiple Attestation and the Historical Jesus
d: How the criterion of multiple attestation works, what it establishes about Jesus, and why scholars from Hooker to Keith warn against over-relying on it.

t: Philippians 2:6-11: The Carmen Christi and Christ's Deity
d: A deep dive into Philippians 2:6-11 — the pre-Pauline "Christ-hymn," its Greek, the harpagmos debate, and the case for an astonishingly early high Christology.

t: The HANDS Framework: Is Jesus God in the New Testament?
d: The HANDS framework — Honors, Attributes, Names, Deeds, Seat — and how the New Testament places Jesus within the unique identity of the one God.

t: The Titles of Jesus: Son of Man, Messiah, Lord
d: How the New Testament's titles for Jesus — Son of Man, Messiah, Lord — read in their Jewish context, reach into the very identity of the God of Israel.

t: The Virgin Birth and Isaiah 7:14: What the Gospels Claim and What History Can Say
d: A careful look at the virginal conception of Jesus in Matthew and Luke, the Isaiah 7:14 debate, pagan-parallel and silence objections, and what it does and doesn't establish.

t: Was the Resurrection Borrowed from Pagan Myths?
d: The early Christian claim that Jesus rose bodily fits no Jewish or pagan category. Examining the dying-rising-gods parallel and what uniqueness proves.

t: The Minimal Facts Argument: Building on Ground Even Sceptics Concede
d: How Habermas and Licona argue the resurrection from a handful of facts critical scholars across the spectrum accept — the method, its power, and its limits.

t: Was Jesus Given an Honourable Burial? Joseph of Arimathea and the Empty Tomb
d: Why the burial of Jesus by Joseph of Arimathea — a Sanhedrist — is one of the best-attested facts about him, and how Crossan's "eaten by dogs" thesis is answered.

t: The Empty Tomb: How Strong Is the Case That the Grave Was Found Empty?
d: The lines of evidence for the empty tomb — enemy attestation, the women witnesses, the Jerusalem factor, Joseph of Arimathea — and the sceptical case answered.

t: They Saw Him: The Appearances and Why the Hallucination Theory Fails
d: Multiple independent sources, group and individual witnesses, doubters and enemies — and why subjective-vision theories cannot explain the post-resurrection appearances.

t: Why Did Paul Convert to Christianity? His Conversion and the Early Creed
d: Why a hostile witness like Saul of Tarsus — no grief, no wish, no expectation — resists naturalistic explanation, and how his testimony anchors the resurrection creed.

t: The Conversion of the Sceptics: James, the Brother of Jesus
d: Why the conversion of James — Jesus's sceptical brother turned martyr-leader of the Jerusalem church — is one of the resurrection's strongest historical clues.

t: Why Did the Disciples Believe Jesus Rose? The Origin of Resurrection Faith
d: Even sceptics grant the disciples sincerely believed they saw the risen Jesus. The hard question is how that belief arose at all in a first-century Jewish world.

t: Post-Resurrection Behaviour: Reading Backward From What the Church Became
d: The transformation of the disciples and the strange new shape of the early church are data. What cause best explains them?

t: Are the New Testament Manuscripts Reliable?
d: How 5,800+ Greek manuscripts and a short gap to the originals show the text was accurately preserved — and why preservation is not the same as truth.

t: How Early Was the New Testament Written?
d: The 1 Corinthians 15 creed, the silence of Acts, and the patristic testimony — and the honest, genuinely contested case for an early date.

t: Were the Gospels Based on Eyewitnesses?
d: Bauckham's named witnesses, the inclusio, and the onomastic data against the form critics — what eyewitness rootedness does and does not prove.

t: Do the Gospel Names Fit First-Century Palestine?
d: Why the frequency of names in the Gospels matches the real Palestinian-Jewish pattern Tal Ilan documented — a fingerprint hard to fake from far away.

t: The Jewishness of Jesus and the Gospels
d: How deeply the Gospels are rooted in Second-Temple Judaism — and why that argues for early, authentic tradition rather than late Hellenistic invention.

t: Undesigned Coincidences in the Gospels
d: The incidental, unplanned ways the accounts dovetail — from Paley to Lydia McGrew — and Richard Carrier's literary-dependence reply, answered on the merits.

t: Is the Bible Internally Consistent?
d: The hardest alleged contradictions — the genealogies, Judas's death, the day of the crucifixion — faced honestly, with Ehrman steelmanned.

t: How Was the Bible's Canon Decided?
d: Why the canon was recognised, not invented at Nicaea — the criteria, the disputed books, and the Catholic/Protestant difference, fairly stated.

t: What Does Archaeology Really Prove About the Bible?
d: The inscriptions and sites that confirm the Bible's historical setting, the honest disputes (Exodus, conquest, monarchy), and what the spade cannot settle.

t: What the Dead Sea Scrolls Prove About the Bible
d: How the Great Isaiah Scroll shows the Old Testament was faithfully transmitted — and the textual pluriformity an honest case must concede.

t: Did Jesus Fulfill Old Testament Prophecy?
d: The striking cases (Isaiah 53, Daniel 9) and the hard ones (Hosea 11:1, Isaiah 7:14) — prediction versus typology, without the dubious probability math.

t: Does the Big Bang Point to God?
d: The evidence that the universe began — expansion, the CMB, the Borde–Guth–Vilenkin theorem — and the honest limits of the move from a beginning to a cause.

t: Is the Cosmos Purposeful?
d: Beyond the constants: the low-entropy beginning, the intelligibility of nature, and the discoverability of the world — and whether together they suggest a purpose.

t: Where Do the Laws of Nature Come From?
d: Why the cosmos is law-governed and intelligible at all, the contingency of the particular laws, and the deflationary “brute fact” reply answered on the merits.

t: Why Does Mathematics Describe the Universe?
d: Wigner’s “unreasonable effectiveness,” the cases where pure mathematics predicted physics, and the selection and anthropic replies, fairly weighed.

t: The Origin of Life: Evidence and Design
d: The unsolved problem of abiogenesis and biological information — the RNA world, the design inference, and the god-of-the-gaps charge answered honestly.

t: The Cambrian Explosion and the Case for Design
d: The rapid appearance of animal body plans, Meyer’s information argument, and the full mainstream paleontological reply — contested, not consensus.

t: Is Earth a Privileged Planet?
d: The Gonzalez–Richards claim that habitability and discoverability correlate — the eclipse case, the Copernican objection, and what it does not show.

t: The Trinity in the New Testament
d: The triadic texts, the deity of the Son and the Spirit, and how the doctrine is the necessary synthesis of the New Testament’s own data — with the unitarian reading answered.

t: The Deity and Personhood of the Holy Spirit
d: The “shy” third person: why the Spirit is a person and not an “active force,” why he is fully God, and how Constantinople 381 settled it — the Jehovah’s Witness and biblical-unitarian case answered.

t: Why Every Trinity Analogy Fails
d: Water, egg, shamrock, sun — each analogy, pressed, teaches a specific heresy (modalism, partialism, Arianism). The Shield of the Trinity, why they all fail, and what to say instead.

t: Old Testament Foundations of the Trinity
d: Plurality-within-unity in the Hebrew Scriptures — Genesis 1:26, the Angel of the LORD, Word, Wisdom, Spirit, Daniel 7 — read without anachronism, as preparation rather than proof.

t: The Shema and the Trinity
d: “The LORD is one” — and Paul’s astonishing reworking of it in 1 Corinthians 8:6. Why the Trinity affirms the Shema rather than breaking it (without the echad over-reach).

t: The Early Church and the Trinity
d: How the pre-Nicene church already worshipped Father, Son, and Spirit — and what Nicaea actually did. The “Constantine invented it” myth answered with the real history.

t: What Distinguishes the Persons?
d: Same being, one will, one power — so what makes Father, Son, and Spirit distinct? The three classical rules: relations of origin, perichoresis (mutual indwelling), and inseparable operations.

t: The Eternal Generation of the Son
d: “Begotten, not made” — an eternal relation of origin, not a beginning or an inequality. Its biblical basis, patristic roots, and the modern evangelical debate, fairly stated.

t: A Philosophical Defence of the Trinity
d: Is one God in three persons a contradiction? The orthodox claim stated precisely, the failure modes named, and the leading models — social and Latin — weighed even-handedly.

t: Answering Modalism: One God in Three Persons
d: Why Father, Son, and Spirit are not three masks of one person — the Oneness/Sabellian case steelmanned and answered, without overcorrecting into tritheism.

t: Answering Islamic Objections to the Trinity
d: Tawhid, shirk, and the Qur’an’s “say not three” — what Christians do and do not claim, answered charitably and accurately, with Islam represented fairly.

t: Answering Jehovah’s Witnesses on the Trinity
d: John 1:1 and “a god,” “firstborn of creation,” “the Father is greater” — the Watchtower case met on the Greek and the text, graciously and on the merits.

t: Answering Mormonism on the Godhead
d: Three separate beings, exaltation, a plurality of gods — the LDS Godhead from its own sources, and the case for one God, eternally God, in three coequal persons.

t: The Islamic Dilemma
d: The Quran affirms the Torah and Gospel as God’s revelation — which traps the case whether the Bible is called reliable or corrupted. Tahrif handled precisely.

t: Was Jesus a Muslim? The Christian Response
d: Muslims often say Jesus was a Muslim — a submitter to God. The equivocation on ‘muslim’, the first-century Jewish Jesus, the divine claims no submitter could make, and his submission to the Father answered without compromising the Trinity.

t: Jesus in the Quran
d: What the Quran’s Isa affirms (virgin birth, miracles, Messiah, ‘a Word from God’) and denies (deity, the crucifixion) — and why the cross is historical bedrock.

t: Is the Quran Perfectly Preserved?
d: The Uthmanic recension, the Companion codices, and the Sana’a palimpsest — what the history does and does not support, fairly and without overstatement.

t: The Variant Readings (Qira’at)
d: The ahruf hadith, the canonical readings (Hafs vs Warsh), and where they differ in meaning — the popular ‘one perfect text’ claim, examined.

t: Contradictions in the Qur’an
d: Surah 4:82 invites the contradiction test — applied honestly: the Lot command that differs between certified editions, King vs Owner, and the classic internal tensions, each steelmanned first.

t: The Preservation Promise vs the Lost Verses
d: Surah 15:9 set against the Islamic reports of the stoning verse, the suckling verse, and naskh al-tilawa — with the weak reports flagged honestly.

t: Abrogation (Naskh)
d: 2:106, the three classical types, the qibla and ‘sword’ verses, and the scholarly dispute over how many verses are abrogated — the eternal Word that revises itself.

t: Hadith Science
d: Isnad criticism, al-Bukhari’s sifting, the chronological gap, and the Goldziher–Schacht–Motzki debate — can the science bear the weight placed on it?

t: The Sira: The Sources for Muhammad
d: Ibn Ishaq via Ibn Hisham, the thin 7th-century record, and the revisionists — a real historical Muhammad, with the detailed biography held loosely.

t: Does the Bible Predict Muhammad?
d: Deuteronomy 18, the Paraclete (parakletos, not periklytos), and the ‘Ahmad’ claims — the Muslim case stated fairly and answered in context.

t: Wahy vs Incarnation
d: Two models of how God reaches us: a book sent down, or the Word made flesh. A comparative, irenic case that the Quran’s true analogue is Christ, not the Bible.

t: The Eternal Word Dilemma
d: The Mihna, the createdness controversy, and the Ash’ari resolution — how an eternal, uncreated divine Word echoes the logic of the Logos of John 1.

t: Tawhid and the Problem of Eternal Love
d: Can a strictly unitarian God be eternally loving? The divine name al-Wadud, the beloved love requires, and how the Trinity answers — argued by depth, not attack.

t: Is there any evidence that God exists?
d: Yes - several independent lines of evidence point to God: the universe's beginning, its fine-tuning, morality, and human consciousness.

t: What caused the universe?
d: Modern cosmology traces the universe to a finite beginning - and whatever begins to exist has a cause that is timeless, spaceless, and powerful.

t: Why is there something rather than nothing?
d: Everything in the universe could have failed to exist. That points to a necessary being that exists by its own nature - the reason anything exists.

t: Is the universe fine-tuned for life?
d: The constants of physics sit in an extraordinarily narrow life-permitting range. Design is a serious explanation alongside chance and necessity.

t: Where do morality and human rights come from without God?
d: If some things are truly wrong - not just disliked - that binding moral law points to a moral lawgiver beyond human opinion.

t: Can you be good without believing in God?
d: Of course atheists can be good people. The real question isn't whether you can act morally without God, but what grounds morality at all.

t: Doesn't science disprove God?
d: Science studies how the physical world works; it isn't equipped to rule out a Creator. Many founders of modern science were devout believers.

t: Did Jesus really exist?
d: Virtually all historians - including non-Christians - agree Jesus of Nazareth was a real first-century figure who was crucified under Pilate.

t: Is there evidence that Jesus rose from the dead?
d: A core set of facts most historians accept - the crucifixion, empty tomb, appearances, and the disciples' transformation - is best explained by the resurrection.

t: Wasn't the resurrection just a legend that grew over time?
d: The resurrection claim traces to within a few years of the cross - far too early for legend. 1 Corinthians 15 preserves a creed scholars date to within ~5 years.

t: Why does it matter that women were the first witnesses to the empty tomb?
d: In a culture that discounted female testimony, no one inventing a story would make women the key witnesses. It's a fingerprint of history, not fiction.

t: Could the disciples have stolen Jesus' body?
d: The theft theory was the earliest counter-claim - but it concedes the tomb was empty, and it can't explain why the disciples died for what they'd know was a lie.

t: Did the disciples just hallucinate the risen Jesus?
d: Hallucinations are private and individual. The resurrection appearances were to groups, on different occasions, including skeptics - and the tomb was still empty.

t: Why did Paul go from persecuting Christians to dying for Jesus?
d: Paul was an enemy of the church with everything to lose. His sudden, costly reversal - he said he saw the risen Jesus - is hard to explain away.

t: Has the Bible been changed over the centuries?
d: No core teaching rests on a disputed line. With 5,800+ Greek manuscripts, scholars can trace and correct copying differences - the text is well preserved.

t: Can we trust the New Testament manuscripts?
d: The New Testament is the best-attested document of antiquity by a wide margin - 5,800+ Greek copies versus a handful for most ancient works.

t: Aren't there contradictions in the Bible?
d: Most alleged contradictions are differences in perspective or emphasis - exactly what independent eyewitness accounts look like, not copying errors.

t: Were the Gospels written by eyewitnesses?
d: The Gospels are rooted in eyewitness testimony, written within living memory of the events, with incidental details that fit first-century Palestine.

t: Is there archaeological evidence for the Bible?
d: Hundreds of finds - the Pilate Stone, Caiaphas ossuary, Hezekiah's tunnel, and more - confirm people, places, and events the Bible describes.

t: Did Jesus actually claim to be God?
d: Yes - in word and action. He took God's own name ("Before Abraham was, I AM"), forgave sins, accepted worship, and said "I and the Father are one."

t: Isn't the Trinity a contradiction?
d: No. The Trinity says God is one in being and three in persons - different categories. It would only contradict if it claimed one God and three Gods.

t: Wasn't Jesus just a good moral teacher?
d: Given his claims to deity, "good teacher" isn't on the menu. As C.S. Lewis argued, a man saying such things is either Lord, liar, or lunatic.

t: Did the church invent Jesus' divinity at the Council of Nicaea?
d: No. Christians worshipped Jesus as divine from the earliest decades. Nicaea (325) didn't invent his deity - it defended language to express it.

t: Did Jesus fulfill Isaiah 53?
d: Isaiah 53, written centuries before Jesus and preserved in the Dead Sea Scrolls, describes a pierced, silent, buried-then-vindicated servant who suffers for others.

t: If God is good, why is there so much suffering?
d: This is the hardest question in faith - and it cuts both ways: calling suffering truly evil assumes a real standard of good that points back to God.

t: How can a loving God send people to hell?
d: Hell is best understood not as God forcing people away, but as God honoring the freedom of those who finally refuse him. Love that can't be refused isn't love.

t: Could evil actually be evidence FOR God?
d: Surprisingly, yes. To call something objectively evil presupposes a real standard of good - and that standard is hard to ground without God.

t: Where is God when I'm hurting?
d: Christianity's answer isn't a formula but a Person - a God who entered suffering himself in Jesus and promises to be near the brokenhearted.

t: Aren't all religions basically the same?
d: They share some ethics, but make contradictory claims about God, humanity, and salvation. They can't all be true - though they can all be examined.

t: Isn't faith just believing without evidence?
d: Biblical faith isn't blind - it means trust based on good reasons. The New Testament repeatedly appeals to evidence, witnesses, and reasoning.

t: Is it okay to have doubts as a Christian?
d: Yes. Doubt isn't the opposite of faith - it's often faith seeking understanding. Scripture is full of honest questions, and the answer is to investigate, not suppress.

t: Has the Quran been changed or corrupted?
d: Muslims are taught the Quran is perfectly preserved, but its textual history — competing canonical readings and the Uthmanic standardisation — tells a more complicated story.

t: Was Jesus a Muslim?
d: Muslims say Jesus was a Muslim — a submitter to God. He did submit to the Father, but he also claimed and accepted what no mere submitter could: to forgive sins and receive worship.

t: Does the Bible predict Muhammad?
d: Islam claims the Bible foretold Muhammad (the 'Paraclete' of John, the prophet of Deuteronomy 18). Read in context, these passages point elsewhere.

t: If God made everything, who made God?
d: If God made everything, who made God? The question misreads the claim: whatever begins to exist has a cause, and God did not begin to exist.

t: Was Jesus copied from pagan myths like Horus and Mithras?
d: Was the resurrection borrowed from pagan dying-and-rising gods like Osiris or Mithras? The alleged parallels are late, loose, and don't fit the history.

t: Who decided which books are in the Bible?
d: Who decided the books of the Bible? Not Constantine or Nicaea. The core was received early and recognized over centuries by principled criteria.

t: Do Jehovah's Witnesses believe Jesus is God?
d: Do Jehovah's Witnesses believe Jesus is God? No. They hold he is God's first and greatest creature. Here is that view answered with respect.

t: Is the Mormon Jesus the same as the Jesus of the Bible?
d: No - the name is shared, but Latter-day Saint teaching sets Jesus within a different doctrine of God than Scripture and the creeds confess.

t: If Jesus is God, why did he pray to God and say he didn't know the day or hour?
d: If Jesus is God, why did he pray and say he didn't know the day or hour? Because in the incarnation the Son took on a real human nature.

t: Where is the Trinity in the Bible?
d: Where is the Trinity in the Bible? The word is later, but the data isn't: one God, with Father, Son, and Spirit each God and genuinely distinct.

t: What is the minimal facts argument for the resurrection?
d: What is the minimal facts argument? It builds the case for the resurrection only on facts even skeptical scholars grant, then weighs explanations.

t: Why did the disciples die for their belief in the resurrection?
d: Why did the disciples die for the resurrection? Their willingness to suffer shows sincerity, not that they were lying. But what does that prove?

t: Do Muslims and Christians worship the same God?
d: Do Muslims and Christians worship the same God? No. Islam explicitly denies the Son, and Scripture ties having the Father to having the Son - so the two describe God incompatibly.

t: Why were some gospels left out of the Bible?
d: Gospels like Thomas, Peter, and Judas were left out mainly for chronology, not a power play: they were written generations too late to be apostolic.

t: Is Jesus the same person as Michael the Archangel?
d: Jehovah's Witnesses identify Jesus with Michael the archangel. But Jude 9 and Hebrews 1 place the Son categorically above the angels.

t: Are Mormons Christians?
d: No — not in the sense that matters most. Latter-day Saints self-identify as Christian, but their doctrine of God departs at the root from the historic Nicene creed shared by Catholics, Orthodox, and Protestants.

t: Are Jehovah's Witnesses Christians?
d: No - not in the sense that matters most. Jehovah's Witnesses self-identify as Christian, but they deny the full deity of Christ and the Trinity confessed by the historic Nicene creed.

t: Does the Big Bang point to God?
d: The Big Bang is strong evidence the universe began - but a beginning is not by itself a proof of God. It is one step; the rest takes further reasoning.

t: Did Jesus die on the cross, or does the Quran deny it?
d: On its dominant reading the Quran denies Jesus was crucified. Yet his death by crucifixion is the best-attested fact about his life in history.

t: Is Jesus really 'a god' in John 1:1 (New World Translation)?
d: The New World Translation renders John 1:1 'the Word was a god.' Greek grammar points instead to a qualitative sense: the Word fully shares God's nature.

t: Can science explain the origin of life without God?
d: There is currently no consensus scientific mechanism for life's origin. A design inference is one contested option - not proof, and not a rejection of evolution.

t: Does the multiverse explain away the fine-tuning of the universe?
d: The multiverse is a serious rival to design, not a scientific refutation of fine-tuning - and it seems to need fine-tuning of its own to get going.

t: Isn't God just wishful thinking?
d: Wanting God to exist says nothing about whether he does - that cuts both ways. And our deepest longings may be a suggestive pointer, not proof either way.

t: How many Old Testament prophecies did Jesus fulfill?
d: You'll see numbers like 300 and odds of 1 in 10^17, but that statistic is unsound. The real case is cumulative convergence, not a math proof.

t: Is the virgin birth credible?
d: A modest, honest case: the virgin birth rests on two independent Gospels and an early, embarrassing tradition - real weight, but held by faith, not proof.

t: Is there any evidence for Jesus outside the Bible?
d: Yes - several non-Christian sources from within a century of Jesus mention him, including the Roman historian Tacitus and the Jewish historian Josephus.

t: When were the gospels written?
d: Most scholars date the four gospels between roughly AD 65 and 95 - within living memory of Jesus, and resting on testimony that traces back even earlier.

t: Is there any evidence that the tomb was empty?
d: Yes - the empty tomb is supported by multiple independent sources, the embarrassing detail of female witnesses, and the fact that Jesus's own opponents admitted it.

t: What is the evidence that Jesus is God?
d: The earliest Christians - monotheistic Jews - worshipped Jesus, gave him God's own titles and works, and did so within years of the crucifixion, not centuries later.

t: What makes Christianity different from other religions?
d: Christianity is unique in two ways: it rests on a testable historical claim (the resurrection), and it teaches that God comes down to us by grace rather than us earning our way up.

t: What do the Dead Sea Scrolls prove about the Bible?
d: Discovered in 1947, they show the Old Testament text was copied with remarkable accuracy over a thousand years - confirming careful preservation, though not the truth of its contents.

t: What are Thomas Aquinas's Five Ways?
d: They are five arguments for God from motion, causation, contingency, degrees of perfection, and the order of nature - each reasoning from features of the world to a necessary first cause.

t: Why do the gospel resurrection accounts differ?
d: The differences are in peripheral details - the kind of variation expected from independent eyewitnesses - while all four agree on the core: Jesus was crucified, buried, his tomb was found empty, and he appeared alive.

t: Why is Jesus called the Son of God?
d: Not because he was created or born as a lesser divine being - the title marks his unique, eternal relationship to the Father and his sharing in the one divine nature.

t: Is the Trinity like water, ice, and steam?
d: No - that popular analogy actually describes an ancient error called modalism. The Trinity is not one person in three modes, but one God existing eternally as three distinct persons.

t: Why do bad things happen to good people?
d: The wicked prosper and the innocent suffer - it feels backwards. But that very sense of 'unfair' assumes a moral order that fits a just God.

t: Why doesn't God heal everyone or answer every prayer?
d: Unanswered prayer is a real ache, not a sign of weak faith. Prayer isn't a vending machine - the healing God promises is the world's full renewal.

t: Why does God allow natural disasters?
d: Natural disasters are the hardest form of the problem of evil - and Christians shouldn't pretend otherwise. Honest answers, not spin.

t: Isn't Christianity just true because you were raised in it?
d: Where you're born shapes what you believe - but that's the genetic fallacy. How a belief arises says nothing about whether it's actually true.

t: If God is real, why does he seem hidden?
d: If God is loving, why is he not obvious? A serious look at divine hiddenness - forced proof isn't love, and hiddenness isn't the same as absence.

t: What about those who have never heard of Jesus?
d: Billions never heard of Jesus. Scripture holds Christ as the only way and God as perfectly just - and calls us to real humility about the rest.

t: Can a Christian believe in evolution?
d: Faithful Christians hold a range of views on evolution. Genesis answers who and why; science asks how - they're different questions, not rivals.

t: Does the Bible condone slavery?
d: An honest answer: the Bible condemned man-stealing, regulated debt-servitude, and planted the truths - imago Dei, Exodus, Galatians 3:28 - that undid slavery.

t: Is Islam true?
d: The sharpest test comes from inside the Quran itself: it affirms the Torah and Gospel as God's revelation, yet contradicts the Bible those scriptures contain.

t: Are there contradictions in the Quran?
d: Surah 4:82 stakes the Quran's divine origin on containing no contradiction. Applied honestly on its own terms, that showpiece test does not come back empty.

t: Was Muhammad a true prophet?
d: History secures a real Muhammad and a real seventh-century movement. But the vivid biography rests on late, edited sources, and should be held loosely.

t: What does the Quran say about Jesus?
d: The Quran says a surprising amount about Jesus - his virgin birth, miracles, the title Messiah. But it denies the two things the Gospel cannot do without.

t: Is the New World Translation accurate?
d: The New World Translation bends the Greek to fit Watchtower doctrine: 'the Word was a god' in John 1:1, and inserting 'other' four times into Colossians 1.

t: What is the Daniel 70 weeks prophecy?
d: Daniel 9's 'seventy weeks' expects an Anointed One to come and be 'cut off' within the Second Temple era - a striking convergence, if you avoid overclaiming.

t: Did Jesus really rise from the dead?
d: The case rests on facts even skeptical scholars grant: Jesus' crucifixion, the disciples' experiences, and a creed dated within a few years of the cross.

t: How do we know the Bible is true?
d: An honest distinction first: the manuscripts prove the Bible was accurately preserved - not that its contents are true. Those two questions are argued separately.

t: Is there scientific evidence for God?
d: The physics is real and largely uncontested: the constants sit in a razor-thin life-permitting range. What it means is a philosophical inference, contested.

t: What is the Kalam cosmological argument?
d: Two premises: whatever begins to exist has a cause, and the universe began to exist. The conclusion points to a transcendent, powerful cause of the cosmos.

t: Does evolution disprove God?
d: No. Even a fully successful evolution describes a mechanism - it cannot say why there is a universe, with life-permitting laws, for any mechanism to work in.

t: Is Jesus God, or just a prophet?
d: 'Just a prophet' is the Muslim and JW view - but the earliest Christians did with Jesus what no Jew ever did with a prophet: worshiped and prayed to him.

t: What is the moral argument for God?
d: The moral argument for God, explained: not the claim that atheists can't be good, but that objective moral duties need a real ground beyond human opinion.

t: What is the ontological argument?
d: The ontological argument, from Anselm to Plantinga: the bold claim that the greatest conceivable being must exist — its real power and its honest limits.

t: Is consciousness evidence for God?
d: Does the inner light of consciousness point to God? Why the 'hard problem' strains a purely physical world — and what the theistic inference can and can't show.

t: What is the argument from desire?
d: C. S. Lewis's argument from desire: if we carry a longing no earthly thing can satisfy, the most probable explanation is that we were made for another world.

t: What is the Leibniz contingency argument?
d: Leibniz's contingency argument asks not when the universe began but why anything exists at all — and why a necessary, transcendent ground is the best answer.

t: Did the universe have a beginning?
d: Did the universe have a beginning? Expansion, the microwave background, and the Borde–Guth–Vilenkin theorem point to a cosmic past boundary — and to a cause.

t: Does beauty point to God?
d: Does beauty point to God? Why the elegance of nature, art, and even mathematics fits a universe with an Author better than one of blind, purposeless matter.

t: Doesn't the Trinity mean three Gods?
d: Doesn't the Trinity mean three Gods? Why it remains a doctrine of one God — and how Paul, a Pharisee, placed Jesus inside Israel's own creed, the Shema.

t: What does 'Son of Man' mean?
d: What does 'Son of Man' mean? Not a title of humility but Daniel 7's heavenly figure — the claim that got Jesus charged with blasphemy at his own trial.

t: Did Jesus appear to 500 witnesses?
d: Did Jesus appear to 500 witnesses? The 1 Corinthians 15 creed dates to within a few years of the cross — far too early for the resurrection to be legend.

t: Can we trust the hadith?
d: Can we trust the hadith? A fair look at Islam's isnad science, the two-century gap, and why even its defenders call its certainty probabilistic, not proven.

t: Why are there four Gospels?
d: Why are there four Gospels? To historians, independent witnesses are a gift: how multiple attestation makes the broad shape of Jesus' life hard to dismiss.

t: Does naturalism undermine reason?
d: Naturalism traces the mind to causes selected for survival, not truth - so can it justify trusting reason at all? Lewis and Plantinga press the point.

t: Is religious experience evidence for God?
d: Across every culture, vast numbers report meeting God. Swinburne and Alston argue that testimony counts as genuine evidence - though not coercive proof.

t: Is Earth specially designed for life?
d: The same rare conditions that make Earth habitable also make it an unusually good observatory. Gonzalez and Richards argue that pairing points to purpose.

t: Is mathematics evidence for God?
d: Why should abstract math invented for its own sake turn out to run the cosmos? Wigner called it a miracle; it fits a rational mind better than blind matter.

t: What are undesigned coincidences?
d: One Gospel raises a puzzle another casually answers, with neither noticing - the interlocking fingerprint of independent witnesses to the same real events.

t: Do the names in the Gospels show eyewitness testimony?
d: The personal names in the Gospels match first-century Jewish Palestine with a statistical fidelity almost impossible to fake from far away or long after.

t: Was Jesus really buried in a tomb?
d: Jesus' burial by Joseph of Arimathea is among the best-attested facts we have about him - early, multiply attested, and awkward enough to be authentic.

t: Did Jesus predict his own resurrection?
d: The Gospels report Jesus foretelling his death and vindication. Critics say that is hindsight - but the awkward, veiled sayings still resist easy invention.

t: Did Jesus empty himself of his deity? (Philippians 2)
d: Philippians 2 says Christ "emptied himself" - but the Greek names nothing he emptied himself of. He veiled his glory by adding humanity, not shedding deity.

t: Is the Trinity in the Old Testament?
d: The Old Testament does not teach the developed Trinity - but it presents one God whose life is richer than a bare unit, and it anticipates what the NT reveals.

t: What is abrogation (naskh) in the Quran?
d: Naskh is the Islamic doctrine that a later revelation can cancel an earlier one - a mainstream teaching that sits in real tension with the Quran's own claims.

t: Was Jesus sinless?
d: Was Jesus sinless? The earliest Christians - Jews raised to believe all have sinned - made that claim of him alone, and even his enemies found no charge.

t: A Posteriori
d: Knowledge gained from experience or evidence

t: A Priori
d: Knowledge independent of experience or observation

t: Ad Hominem
d: Attacking the person rather than their argument

t: Agnosticism
d: The view that God's existence cannot be known

t: Anthropic Principle
d: The observation that the universe must allow observers to exist

t: Apologetics
d: The discipline of giving reasoned defences of Christian faith

t: Argument from Design
d: The universe shows evidence of intentional design

t: Argument from Reason
d: If materialism is true, we cannot trust our own reasoning

t: Atheism
d: The belief that God does not exist

t: Atonement
d: How Christ's death deals with human sin

t: Begging the Question
d: Assuming what you're trying to prove

t: Burden of Proof
d: Who must prove what in an argument

t: Christology
d: The study of the person and nature of Jesus Christ

t: Compatibilism
d: Free will and determinism can both be true

t: Contingency
d: Something that exists but might not have existed

t: Cosmological Argument
d: The universe's existence points to God

t: Criterion of Embarrassment
d: Details that embarrass the author are more likely to be true

t: Criterion of Multiple Attestation
d: Events confirmed by multiple independent sources

t: Dead Sea Scrolls
d: Ancient manuscripts proving the Bible's textual reliability

t: Deism
d: God created the universe but does not intervene in it

t: Determinism
d: All events are causally necessitated by prior events

t: Empiricism
d: All knowledge comes from sensory experience

t: Epistemology
d: The study of knowledge — how we know what we know

t: Euthyphro Dilemma
d: Is something good because God commands it, or does God command it because it's good?

t: Evidentialism
d: Christian faith should be based on evidence

t: Faith
d: Trust based on evidence and testimony, not blind belief

t: Fallibilism
d: All knowledge claims might be wrong

t: Fine-Tuning Argument
d: The precise calibration of nature's constants suggests design

t: General Revelation
d: Knowledge of God available to all through creation and conscience

t: Historical Method
d: The criteria historians use to evaluate ancient sources

t: Hypothesis
d: A proposed explanation to be tested against evidence

t: Immanence
d: God is present within and throughout creation

t: Incarnation
d: God became human in the person of Jesus

t: Inference to the Best Explanation
d: Choosing the hypothesis that best explains all the evidence

t: Kalam Cosmological Argument
d: The universe began to exist — therefore it has a cause

t: Kenosis
d: Christ's voluntary self-emptying in the Incarnation

t: Logical Fallacy
d: An error in reasoning that makes an argument invalid

t: Metaphysics
d: The study of ultimate reality — what exists and why

t: Minimal Facts Argument
d: Arguing for the resurrection using only data all scholars accept

t: Miracles
d: Events involving divine action that transcend natural processes

t: Monotheism
d: Belief in one God

t: Naturalism
d: Only the natural, physical world exists

t: Necessary Being
d: A being that cannot not exist

t: Ontological Argument
d: God's existence follows from the concept of God alone

t: Pantheism
d: God and the universe are identical

t: Penal Substitution
d: Christ bore the punishment we deserved

t: Postmodernism
d: Scepticism about objective truth and universal claims

t: Presuppositionalism
d: All reasoning begins from foundational commitments

t: Problem of Evil
d: Why would a good God allow suffering and evil?

t: Rationalism
d: Reason alone can establish fundamental truths

t: Reformed Epistemology
d: Belief in God can be rational without argument

t: Relativism
d: Truth and morality vary between cultures or individuals

t: Resurrection
d: Jesus rose bodily from the dead on the third day

t: Scientism
d: Science is the only reliable source of knowledge

t: Sola Scriptura
d: Scripture alone is the supreme authority for Christian faith

t: Straw Man
d: Misrepresenting an opponent's argument to make it easier to attack

t: Supernaturalism
d: Reality includes more than the natural, physical world

t: Tacitus
d: Rome's greatest historian who confirms the crucifixion of Christ

t: Teleological Argument
d: The order and purpose in nature point to a designer

t: Theism
d: Belief in a personal God who created and sustains the universe

t: Theodicy
d: A defence of God's goodness in the face of evil and suffering

t: Transcendence
d: God is above and beyond the created order

t: Trinity
d: One God in three persons: Father, Son, and Holy Spirit

t: Trilemma
d: Lord, Liar, or Lunatic — the three options for Jesus

t: Verification Principle
d: Only empirically verifiable or logically necessary claims are meaningful

t: Worldview
d: The comprehensive framework through which we interpret reality
